summary: Paul Graham answers Inc's question by choosing the five founders who most influenced his own thinking about startups. He frames “interesting” as practical influence—people he cites when advising companies—and highlights each founder’s distinctive contribution, from design sensibility to ruthless clarity to talent-first execution. The list doubles as a compact set of startup lessons about taste, talent, focus, and force of will.
key_points: Influence on his own advice is his test for “most interesting.”
Steve Jobs exemplifies enduring influence through design and taste.
TJ Rodgers models brutally candid, pragmatic, idea-pruning thinking.
Larry & Sergey proved a talent-first, hacker-heavy approach can win early.
Paul Buchheit’s focus on making a few users love you is a core startup lesson.
money_quotes: A lot of startup culture is Apple culture.
What makes him unique is his sense of design.
But Google pushed this idea further than anyone had before.
All you need is good hackers.
it's better, initially, to make a small number of users really love you than a large number kind of like you.
key_insight: The founders who matter most are those whose distinctive ways of thinking—about design, talent, focus, and ambition—become reusable lessons for building startups.
